this process explains SEMANTIC DRIFT, how words change their meaning over time

the legibility/specificity trade-off changes as a word is used increasingly to signal group identity and slowly uses its meaning

that's why I use "metamodern", not "integral" (and will change again)

there is a natural progression (similar to @Meaningness' thoughts on subculture evolution) with any (identity) word:

1) hipsters/geeks come up with it

2) more and more ppl use it as a shibboleth for identity (mops)

3) idiots and sociopaths co-opt it

4) inventing a new word

the limits of the trade-off above are both cringe:

maxxing legibility results in tired clichés and maxxing specificity results in being "that guy" who only talks in neologism

the middle ground between them is where social identity is negotiated through semantics

there is a trade-off between LEGIBILITY and SPECIFICITY

everybody understands "spiritual" but because of that, the word is used in so many contexts and loses its meaning at the limit

in addition, a social dimension of using words for coordinating group identity is at play

mostly bc i don't have the answers + making jhanas more accessible seems net positive

while i probably won't practice the jhanas directly anymore for a while, i took a lot from it and already see how it infuses my practice 

thanks for raving about these retreats @nosilverv and @heynibras 

was great to see @m_ashcroft and @tangrenin popping up on zoom as well

yes, jhanas are "just another experience" and i'm still more interested in liberation independent of conditions 

AND raising baseline well-being and cultivating a relaxed, enjoyable state is obviously skillful and conducive to practice (and a good life)

PLEASURE IS GOOD (duh)

i also learned a ton of mental moves (cc @this_is_silvia). some favs:

1) grounding agitation by locating it in the body and releasing downwards 

2) generate curiosity - "did i know this was gonna happen?" 

3) noticing the shape of attention for detecting distraction faster

much more valuable was the general practice of relaxation and cultivating positive mind states (metta, gratitude, joy, love). 

my relationship with my body has also changed significantly - a week later, i still experience all sensations as pleasurable. 

body as bliss machine

unlocking j1 took me most of the week and the ride from into j3-4 seemed pretty natural/easy from there (didn't get any further, not even sure if 3 or 4) 

while these were extremely pleasurable, they were in the same order of magnitude as other peak experiences (drugs, sex, etc)

@jhanatech did a great job of boiling down instructions to the essence, basically

enjoy + relax + observe with curiosity (and unfolding each)

no mention of piti and sukha. the progression of jhanas was only introduced at the end

knowing that stuff going in made progress harder

what ended up working for me was

1) 15 min  progressive relaxation

2) 15 min. tuning into (any) positive feelings

3) 15 min. "mixing" relaxation and pleasure (usually through the breath)

4) shifting to joy specifically and "spreading" piti through the body

i did not think that it was possible to experience jhanas within a week (especially coming from a non-theravada  practice background) 

but i was still craving them, mind you

that mix of doubt + craving was the main obstacle. it happened only once I stopped giving a f (surprise)

ok so i went on a @jhanatech retreat last week and not only got to experience some nice feels but also changed some of my outlook on meditation practice 

overall, the retreat was extremely well facilitated and supported, props to the team (the hype is real)

Decisions on product, business models, etc., for disruptive technologies can significantly impact society.

 Ethical dilemmas arise with each technological innovation, opening up important and fascinating implications.

There's a feeling of participating in history, of shaping how the technological possibilities will manifest. 

With each innovation, we’re carving out a new future. 

And each time, it feels like a chance to witness the future leaking out from the present.

Tech Rapture is also about the joy of being right. 

Many, including me, knew self-driving cars were coming. 

There's an undeniable thrill in being able to say, "I told you so." 

It's the joy of being an early adopter, of riding the waves of innovation.

Tech Rapture is for the nerds. 

It’s about understanding how years of research for deep neural nets, image recognition, data pipelines & more, resulted in that autonomous car driving me around. 

The deeper the knowledge, the greater the wonder.
